summary: Jeff Atwood argues that the poor quality of Windows default applets like Notepad, Calculator, and Paint reflects badly on Microsoft's overall commitment to quality. He proposes that Microsoft should license better third-party alternatives rather than ship decade-old, minimalist tools. Using HyperTerminal as a successful precedent, he outlines a win-win scenario where customers get better tools, third-party developers get sales opportunities, and Microsoft developers can focus on core OS work. The post criticizes the 'either-or' mindset that treats bundled utilities as unworthy of investment.
key_points: Default bundled applications matter because they're what 99.9% of users actually encounter on any given Windows machine
The quality of small utilities like Notepad and Calculator reflects the overall care put into the operating system
Microsoft successfully licensed HyperTerminal from Hilgraeve for Windows 95, proving the third-party licensing model works
Licensing better applets would benefit customers (better tools), developers (upsell opportunities), and Microsoft (focus on core OS)
Notepad shipping virtually unchanged from Windows 95 to Vista represents an unconscionable lack of progress
money_quotes: The applets that ship in the box with the operating system are, like it or not, the defaults.

But isn't the fit and finish of little applets like these – Notepad, Calculator, Character Map, Paint, Disk Cleanup, Compressed Folders, and dozens of others – indicative of the care and design that goes into the entire operating system?
If Microsoft can't be bothered to bundle a version of Notepad that has basic amenities like a toolbar, what hope does the rest of the operating system have?
The idea that we'll be viewing readme.txt files into 2010 with nothing but no-frills Notepad is unconscionable.
Why doesn't Microsoft simply license a better notepad and bundle it with Vista?
A third party whose entire business revolves around a calculator replacement will have a deeper, more vested interest in building a great calculator applet than any Microsoft employee ever will.
key_insight: The quality of default bundled utilities reveals an organization's true commitment to user experience, and outsourcing to specialists often produces better results than half-hearted in-house efforts.
